eli3: The Central Limit Theorem is one of statistics' most powerful results: averages of many random variables tend toward a Normal distribution, regardless of the original distribution. Flip a biased coin many times, sum the results — the distribution of sums becomes bell-shaped.

More precisely: if X₁, X₂, … are i.i.d. with mean μ and variance σ², then √n(X̄ₙ - μ) → N(0, σ²). The √n scaling is crucial — without it, the variance would shrink to zero; with it, we get a stable non-degenerate limit.

This explains why Normal distributions appear everywhere: measurement errors, heights, test scores, stock returns (approximately). It's the foundation of confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and much of classical statistics.

whenWhy.title: Confidence intervals and hypothesis testing
whenWhy.body: CLT justifies using Normal approximations for sample means. 95% CI: X̄ ± 1.96σ/√n. Z-tests and t-tests rely on Normality of sample means. A/B testing statistics use Normal approximation for proportions.

whenWhy.title: Statistical estimation and inference
whenWhy.body: Maximum likelihood estimators are asymptotically Normal (under regularity conditions). This enables Wald tests, profile likelihood intervals, and large-sample inference in complex models.

whenWhy.title: Error analysis and quality control
whenWhy.body: Manufacturing tolerances assume Normal distribution of errors (sum of many small independent errors). Six Sigma methodology assumes Normal. Control charts use CLT-based limits.

whenWhy.title: Finance and risk modeling
whenWhy.body: Returns are often modeled as Normal (though heavy tails exist). Portfolio theory assumes multivariate Normal. Option pricing (Black-Scholes) assumes log-returns are Normal (so prices are Log-Normal).

deepDiveIntro: The classical CLT says: if you have independent, identically distributed data with a finite mean and variance, then after standardising the sample mean (subtracting the true mean and dividing by the standard error), its distribution converges to a standard normal as sample size grows. 'Convergence in distribution' means the CDFs converge pointwise — the probabilities of falling in any interval approach those of the normal distribution.

The basic version assumes identical distributions. Extensions handle the non-identical case (Lindeberg-Feller condition: no single variable can dominate the sum), multivariate data (the sample mean vector converges to a multivariate normal), and even entire stochastic processes (Donsker's theorem: partial sum processes converge to Brownian motion).

deepDive.heading: Classical CLT Statement
deepDive.body: For independent, identically distributed data with finite mean and variance, the standardised sample mean converges in distribution to a standard normal as sample size grows. The square-root-of-n scaling is critical: without it the average would collapse to a spike at the true mean; with it you get a stable bell-curve shape that reveals the rate of convergence.

deepDive.heading: Why It Works: Characteristic Function Proof
deepDive.body: The standard proof uses characteristic functions, which are like Fourier transforms of probability distributions. For the standardised sum, you multiply n copies of the individual characteristic function, take a Taylor expansion around zero, and show that as n grows the product converges to the exponential of negative t-squared over 2 — which is precisely the characteristic function of a standard normal. Lévy's continuity theorem then converts this convergence of characteristic functions into convergence in distribution.

deepDive.heading: Berry-Esseen Theorem
deepDive.body: The CLT is an asymptotic statement — it says the approximation improves as n grows, but does not say how accurate it is at any given sample size. The Berry-Esseen theorem plugs this gap: the maximum error between the true CDF and the normal approximation shrinks at a rate proportional to one over the square root of n, with a constant that depends on the skewness of the distribution. More skewed data needs a larger sample before the normal approximation is reliable.

deepDive.heading: Lindeberg-Feller CLT
deepDive.body: When the variables are independent but not identically distributed, the CLT still holds under the Lindeberg condition: no single variable can contribute an outsized fraction of the total variance. Intuitively, the sum must be the result of many small contributions rather than one or two dominant terms. The Lyapunov condition — that the sum of cubed deviations is small relative to the total variance — is a simpler sufficient condition that is easier to verify in practice.

deepDive.heading: Multivariate and Functional CLT
deepDive.body: The multivariate CLT extends the result to vectors: the standardised sample mean vector converges to a multivariate normal with the population covariance matrix. Donsker's theorem goes further, showing that the entire partial-sum process (not just the final average) converges to Brownian motion. This is the foundation for stochastic calculus, empirical process theory, and goodness-of-fit tests based on the entire empirical CDF.

takeaways: CLT: √n(X̄ₙ - μ) → N(0, σ²). Sample means become Normal.
Holds for i.i.d. with finite variance. Extensions exist for non-i.i.d.
Berry-Esseen: error bound O(1/√n) with constant depending on skewness.
Foundation of classical statistics: confidence intervals, hypothesis tests.
Multivariate CLT for vectors; Functional CLT (Donsker) for processes.

edgeCases.a: (1) Infinite variance: stable distributions (e.g., Cauchy) converge to other stable limits, not Normal. (2) Strong dependence: long-range dependent processes have different scaling (n^H instead of √n, H > 1/2). (3) No Lindeberg condition: one variable dominates (e.g., power law with infinite variance).

edgeCases.q: How large must n be for CLT to apply?
edgeCases.a: Rule of thumb: n ≥ 30 for 'reasonable' distributions. But depends on skewness and tails: symmetric distributions converge faster; highly skewed or heavy-tailed need larger n. Berry-Esseen gives explicit bounds. For proportions: np ≥ 10 and n(1-p) ≥ 10 for Normal approximation.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between CLT and Law of Large Numbers?
edgeCases.a: LLN: X̄ₙ → μ (converges to a constant). CLT: √n(X̄ₙ - μ) → Normal (converges to a distribution). LLN says where; CLT says how fast and with what distribution. Together: X̄ₙ ≈ N(μ, σ²/n) for large n.

sources.label: Durrett — Probability: Theory and Examples (5th ed.), Ch. 3
van der Vaart — Asymptotic Statistics, Ch. 2-3
